questions.q: Suppose $X$ is an integer-valued random variable on a finite sample space with $\mathbb{E}[X] = 7.3$. Which conclusion is forced by the existence principle?
choices: There exists a sample point at which $X \ge 8$
questions.hint: If every sample point had $X \le 7$ then $\mathbb{E}[X] \le 7 < 7.3$.
questions.explain: If every sample point had $X \le 7$ then $\mathbb{E}[X] \le 7 < 7.3$. So at least one sample point gives $X \ge 8$. Nothing is implied about variance or about the distribution of $X$ on other points.

questions.q: Random tournament on $n=6$ vertices: orient each of the $\binom{6}{2}=15$ edges by an independent fair coin. What is the expected number of Hamilton paths?
questions.hint: There are $6! = 720$ permutations.
questions.explain: There are $6! = 720$ permutations. For each, the probability all $5$ consecutive edges agree with the permutation's direction is $2^{-5} = 1/32$. Linearity gives $\mathbb{E}[X] = 720/32 = 22.5$. The existence principle then forces some tournament on $6$ vertices to have $\ge 23$ Hamilton paths.

questions.q: Select the statements that follow purely from the existence principle (random object with positive probability of property $P$ implies a deterministic object with $P$).
choices: There is a tournament on $n$ vertices with at least $\lceil n!/2^{n-1}\rceil$ Hamilton paths
Every tournament on $n$ vertices has at least $n!/2^{n-1}$ Hamilton paths
There exists a $2$-colouring of $K_n$ with no monochromatic $K_k$ whenever $\binom{n}{k} 2^{1-\binom{k}{2}} < 1$
There is a polynomial-time algorithm to construct the colouring of (3)
questions.hint: Items 1 and 3 are direct existence-by-expectation/union-bound statements.
questions.explain: Items 1 and 3 are direct existence-by-expectation/union-bound statements. Item 2 is wrong — the existence principle never says \emph{every} object has the property, only that one does. Item 4 misreads existence as constructiveness; the probabilistic method gives no algorithm by itself.

questions.q: In Erdős's $R(k,k)$ argument, what is the probability that a fixed $k$-subset $S\subseteq[n]$ is monochromatic under a uniformly random $2$-colouring of $K_n$?
questions.hint: $S$ has $\binom{k}{2}$ edges; the probability they all match a chosen colour is $2^{-\binom{k}{2}}$, and there are $2$ colour choices.
questions.explain: $S$ has $\binom{k}{2}$ edges; the probability they all match a chosen colour is $2^{-\binom{k}{2}}$, and there are $2$ colour choices. So $\mathbb{P}(S\text{ monochromatic}) = 2 \cdot 2^{-\binom{k}{2}} = 2^{1-\binom{k}{2}}$.

questions.q: Why does $\binom{n}{k}\,2^{1-\binom{k}{2}} < 1$ imply $R(k,k) > n$?
choices: It says a random colouring is monochromatic with probability $< 1$, hence sometimes not monochromatic
It bounds the expected number of monochromatic $K_k$'s by $< 1$, so by the union bound (or Markov) some colouring has zero such cliques — hence a $2$-colouring of $K_n$ avoids monochromatic $K_k$
It bounds the variance, forcing concentration
It's the same as $n < 2^{k/2}$ by the central limit theorem
questions.hint: $\mathbb{E}[\#\text{mono }K_k] = \binom{n}{k}\,2^{1-\binom{k}{2}}$ by linearity.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[\#\text{mono }K_k] = \binom{n}{k}\,2^{1-\binom{k}{2}}$ by linearity. If this is $<1$ the count is integer-valued with mean $<1$, so it equals $0$ on a positive-probability event. Such a colouring witnesses $R(k,k) > n$.

questions.q: Plug $k=4$, $n=8$ into Erdős's union bound: compute $\binom{8}{4}\,2^{1-\binom{4}{2}}$.
questions.explain: $\binom{8}{4}=70$ and $2^{1-6}=2^{-5}=1/32$, so the bound is $70/32 = 2.1875$. Since this exceeds $1$, Erdős's argument is inconclusive at $(n,k)=(8,4)$ — and indeed $R(4,4)=18$, far above $8$. Trying larger $n$ inflates $\binom{n}{4}$ further, so the union bound $R(k,k) > 2^{k/2}$ only kicks in for moderately large $k$.

questions.q: Linearity of expectation $\mathbb{E}[X_1+\cdots+X_m]=\sum \mathbb{E}[X_i]$ requires which hypothesis on the $X_i$?
questions.hint: Linearity of $\mathbb{E}$ holds for any integrable random variables — independence is irrelevant.
questions.explain: Linearity of $\mathbb{E}$ holds for any integrable random variables — independence is irrelevant. Independence is needed for $\mathbb{E}[XY]=\mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$ and for variance to add.

questions.q: In $G(n,1/2)$ with $n=10$, what is the expected number of independent sets of size exactly $k=4$?
questions.hint: $\mathbb{E}[\#\{4\text{-indep sets}\}] = \binom{10}{4}\cdot 2^{-\binom{4}{2}} = 210 \cdot 2^{-6} = 210/64 = 3.28125$ — the count is non-zero in expectation, so the first-moment method is inconclusive…
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[\#\{4\text{-indep sets}\}] = \binom{10}{4}\cdot 2^{-\binom{4}{2}} = 210 \cdot 2^{-6} = 210/64 = 3.28125$ — the count is non-zero in expectation, so the first-moment method is inconclusive at this $k$. Pushing $k$ up to $\lceil 2\log_2 10\rceil = 7$ drives the expectation below $1$.

questions.q: Which of these statements about linearity of expectation in $G(n,p)$ are correct?
choices: $\mathbb{E}[\#\text{triangles}] = \binom{n}{3} p^3$ only when triangles are independent (which they are not)
If a sum of indicators has expectation $< 1$, then with positive probability it equals $0$
If a sum of indicators has expectation $= 5$, then it equals $5$ on every sample point
questions.hint: Linearity holds with no independence — item 1 is correct, item 2 is the trap.
questions.explain: Linearity holds with no independence — item 1 is correct, item 2 is the trap. Item 3 is the first-moment principle. Item 4 confuses mean with deterministic value; the variable can fluctuate around $5$.

questions.q: What is the structural idea of the alteration (deletion) method?
choices: Resample bad events until none occur
Sample a random object that is almost good, then locally delete elements involved in each defect
Compute the variance and apply Chebyshev
Use the Lovász Local Lemma to skip defects
questions.hint: The alteration method weakens the requirement that the random object outright satisfy property $P$ — instead it tolerates $\beta$ expected defects, then surgically removes one element per defect.
questions.explain: The alteration method weakens the requirement that the random object outright satisfy property $P$ — instead it tolerates $\beta$ expected defects, then surgically removes one element per defect. The cost is $\beta$ in size; the gain is being able to use much denser random objects.

questions.q: In Erdős's high-girth high-chromatic-number proof, what is altered?
choices: Each cycle of length $\le g-1$ has one of its vertices deleted
Each clique has one of its edges deleted
The graph is replaced by its complement
The vertex set is enlarged to absorb defects
questions.hint: Sample $G\sim G(n,p)$ with $p = n^{\theta-1}$, count short cycles in expectation, then delete one vertex per short cycle.
questions.explain: Sample $G\sim G(n,p)$ with $p = n^{\theta-1}$, count short cycles in expectation, then delete one vertex per short cycle. The remaining graph has girth $\ge g$ and the independence number is unchanged or smaller (deletion is monotone), preserving the chromatic-number lower bound.

questions.q: Which of these statements about the alteration method are correct?
choices: It is constructive in the sense that it produces an explicit algorithm to build the object
It is non-constructive: existence is proven, but no efficient construction is implied
It can give better bounds than the basic probabilistic method by tolerating a few defects
It is equivalent to the Lovász Local Lemma
questions.hint: The deletion method is non-constructive (item 2): you know an object exists, but the proof gives no efficient construction.
questions.explain: The deletion method is non-constructive (item 2): you know an object exists, but the proof gives no efficient construction. It often beats the basic method (item 3) because it works with denser/looser random objects. It is not equivalent to the LLL (item 4) — LLL handles many overlapping bad events without alteration; alteration handles few isolated ones.

questions.q: The symmetric LLL states: if each bad event $A_i$ has probability $\le p$ and depends on at most $d$ others, then $\mathbb{P}(\bigcap \bar A_i) > 0$ provided…
questions.hint: The symmetric LLL hypothesis is $e\,p\,(d+1)\le 1$, where $e$ is Euler's constant.
questions.explain: The symmetric LLL hypothesis is $e\,p\,(d+1)\le 1$, where $e$ is Euler's constant. The factor of $e$ comes from the inductive proof's geometric sum bound.

questions.q: What does the LLL gain over the union bound in $k$-SAT?
choices: Both give the same bound but the LLL is faster
The union bound shows satisfiability when $m \le 2^k$ clauses; the LLL shows it when each variable appears in $\le 2^k/(ek)$ clauses regardless of total $m$
The LLL requires the $A_i$ to be mutually independent
questions.hint: The union bound demands $\sum \mathbb{P}(A_i) = m\,2^{-k} < 1$, useless for large $m$.
questions.explain: The union bound demands $\sum \mathbb{P}(A_i) = m\,2^{-k} < 1$, useless for large $m$. The LLL trades the global sum for a local degree: dependence (sharing a variable) of bounded degree suffices. This is what makes LLL the right tool when bad events are sparse but not few.

questions.q: In $4$-SAT, the LLL guarantees satisfiability whenever each variable appears in at most $2^k/(ek)$ clauses with $k=4$. To the nearest integer, what is this threshold?
questions.explain: $2^4/(e\cdot 4) = 16/(4e) = 4/e \approx 1.47$. Rounded down (the integer floor of the threshold), the LLL covers each variable appearing in at most $1$ clause — a weak bound at $k=4$, but the threshold $2^k/(ek)$ grows exponentially in $k$, dominating the union bound for moderate $k$.

questions.q: Let $X_T$ count triangles in $G(n,p)$. The triangle threshold is at:
questions.hint: $\mathbb{E}[X_T] = \binom{n}{3} p^3 \sim n^3 p^3 / 6$.
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[X_T] = \binom{n}{3} p^3 \sim n^3 p^3 / 6$. This stays bounded iff $p = \Theta(1/n)$. For $p \ll 1/n$ the first moment vanishes and there are no triangles a.a.s.; for $p \gg 1/n$ the second moment shows triangles exist a.a.s.

questions.q: The second moment method shows $\mathbb{P}(X = 0)$ is small whenever:
questions.hint: Chebyshev's inequality gives $\mathbb{P}(X = 0)\le \mathbb{P}(|X-\mathbb{E}[X]|\ge\mathbb{E}[X]) \le \operatorname{Var}(X)/\mathbb{E}[X]^2$.
questions.explain: Chebyshev's inequality gives $\mathbb{P}(X = 0)\le \mathbb{P}(|X-\mathbb{E}[X]|\ge\mathbb{E}[X]) \le \operatorname{Var}(X)/\mathbb{E}[X]^2$. So the variance must be $o(\mathbb{E}[X]^2)$ on top of $\mathbb{E}[X]\to\infty$ for this to vanish.

questions.q: Select the statements about $G(n,p)$ thresholds that are correct.
choices: At $p = c/n$ the number of triangles converges in distribution to $\mathrm{Pois}(c^3/6)$
Every monotone graph property has its threshold at $p = 1/n$
questions.hint: Items 1, 2, 3 are foundational facts of Erdős–Rényi.
questions.explain: Items 1, 2, 3 are foundational facts of Erdős–Rényi. Item 4 is wrong — connectivity ($\log n/n$), triangle-containment ($1/n$), and Hamiltonicity ($\log n/n$) all have different thresholds; the threshold depends on the property.

questions.q: Markov's inequality $\mathbb{P}(X \ge a) \le \mathbb{E}[X]/a$ requires:
questions.hint: Markov's inequality is for non-negative $X$ — it is the most elementary tail bound and the foundation for Chebyshev (apply Markov to $(X-\mu)^2$) and Chernoff (apply Markov to $e^{tX}$).
questions.explain: Markov's inequality is for non-negative $X$ — it is the most elementary tail bound and the foundation for Chebyshev (apply Markov to $(X-\mu)^2$) and Chernoff (apply Markov to $e^{tX}$).

questions.q: Hoeffding's inequality for $S_n = X_1 + \cdots + X_n$ with $X_i \in [0,1]$ independent gives:
questions.hint: Hoeffding: independent bounded variables have a sub-Gaussian tail.
questions.explain: Hoeffding: independent bounded variables have a sub-Gaussian tail. Item 1 is Chebyshev (polynomial decay), item 3 is Markov, item 4 is the law of large numbers as a single point — all weaker than Hoeffding's exponential bound.

questions.q: In Shamir–Spencer's concentration for $\chi(G(n,p))$, why does the vertex-exposure martingale have differences bounded by $1$?
choices: Because revealing one new vertex's edges can change the chromatic number by at most $1$ — at worst the new vertex needs a fresh colour
Because $G(n,p)$ is connected with high probability
questions.hint: Adding a single vertex to an existing optimal colouring needs at most one extra colour, so $\chi$ changes by $\le 1$.
questions.explain: Adding a single vertex to an existing optimal colouring needs at most one extra colour, so $\chi$ changes by $\le 1$. The Doob/exposure martingale of $\chi$ conditional on revealed edges thus has $|c_i| \le 1$, and Azuma gives $\mathbb{P}(|\chi - \mathbb{E}\chi| \ge t) \le 2e^{-t^2/(2n)}$.
